instructions:
Each entry has:
  suggestedTags    — tags to add; set approved: false to skip a clip entirely
  classificationFlag — true if the model thinks the classification may be wrong
  suggestedClassification — proposed replacement (review carefully before accepting)
Run audio-audit-apply.js to write approved tag changes to the DB.
Classification changes are flagged for manual review only — apply-script will not auto-change them.

suggestions.title: World On Fired (Ink Spots pitched down and processed) - Loveless Dog

suggestions.title: Journey Of The Magpie (Chopped Conspiracy Theories) - Loveless Dog
suggestions.comments: I created this piece using a Roland SP-404SX sampler and Ableton Live. I mixed various samples of "conspiracy theorists" downloaded from the internet. The piece begins and ends fairly minimally but gets rather dense in the middle.  I am currently adopting the moniker: Loveless Dog

suggestions.title: Infrastructure (Michael Ash Sharbaugh)
suggestions.comments:
This tune -- composed by me (Michael Ash Sharbaugh) -- is one of my fave soundscapes, and is almost entirely made up of 60Hz sine waves; it was to represent an audible representation of the electrical cycles of the United States infrastructure.

Approved — this is a strong fit for DriftConditions. I adjusted the classification from Soundscape / Experimental to Ambient / Experimental / Digital. “Ambient” fits because this is primarily a droney bed-like piece, “Experimental” fits because it’s built as a conceptual sound work, and “Digital” fits because it’s constructed from sine-wave / electronic material. I removed “Soundscape” because that category is usually better for more layered environmental or scene-based audio worlds. I also simplified the tags to focus on reusable sound and subject words like drone, sine-wave, hum, electricity, infrastructure, minimal, and industrial. In DriftConditions, tags work best when they are simple and plentiful — more about texture, subject, and mood, less about project-specific labels or interpretive words like “strange” or long compound tags. Thanks for sending this in — it’s a great addition.
